have to come towards the good enough.

So in terms of that cutoff point, it would be, does it match the tone, the branding, the language?
Is it going to accurately correct within the scope of all that?
And that's how I would determine if it's good enough or not for production.

And if you want to, like, pick something, and can be anything, but in that, either one of those positions or working with writers, you'd describe, like, a content or editorial process that you saw, and you saw an opportunity for improvement, and you kind of built that out, improved it, and kind of walk me through that.
Oh, sure.
So what I found was, emails are a great example in the current company.
When I initially started, emails were very all over the shop.
Sometimes they'd have emojis, sometimes they wouldn't, sometimes things would be bolded sometimes.
interject really quickly, when you say emails, you're talking about, like, a newsletter or email?
sorry, yeah, promotional email, I should be clear about that.

So very much promotions in the sense of, yeah, so, like, monthly emails.
And what I found was, it was just a range of, like, and because of
Resources at the company was very much, well, just grab what we did last year and then just use it again.
And I'm like, that's fine, but like things have changed in the year.
So my thing was, how do I bring this all together in a cohesive narrative?
Particularly what I found was the landing page had different language to the emails all the time.
Because what they did was they separated it before I was there, where it was like, someone does the landing page, someone does the email.
So you're having two different people around two different things.
So my thing was, I went on to one person, because one person can oversee the whole journey for the user.
And my thing, going back to the emails was, we had to set a sort of a template in terms of how the email is structured.
So we want a problem, this is the possible solution, and how we can help with the solution.
And then on top of that, I added the formatting, what we need to format in terms of like, what's bolded, what's italic, and the type of language you use.
So that's how we built that out.
And then I said, okay, now, obviously.
The mandate was like, let's use ChatGTP to create a custom flow so we can speed this up.
So the idea was now training that AI, a custom AI, to generate those emails and those standards.
And then obviously, while doing that, you've got to, like, there's mistakes.
There's always mistakes at first with ChatGTP, ChatGPT, sorry.
So the idea is like sort of ironing that out in the process.
So that was one thing that I've done with the current company.
Where I've tried to get everything on board.
And then now it's reflected in the landing pages.
So a great example would be the copywriter.
We have a disclaimer.
So the idea is you have to put the disclaimer in the email.
But they would write two different disclaimers.
One on the email, one on the landing page.
I'm like, no, no, you can't do this.
The customer will be confused.
You're just going to keep it one.
So my thing would be like, let's just keep a consistency across the board.
And that's been one of the initiatives I've taken over the last two years of the company.
Yeah, that's great.

Refining it.
What was the hardest bit?

Oh, really?
Or either?
Basically, like for you, of course, but what was the hardest part in implementing that, making the changes in here?
So I would say the hardest part was sort of trying to get everyone on board that we can have that as an agreement.
Because it's one thing for me to take the initiative.
There's another thing for me to speak to other people, to my team, and other managers and be like, this is what I want.
And working within them, working together with them, because I can just say, yeah, to the copywriters, go ahead and do this.
But if someone along catches it and it's like, well, I don't like this, then you're sort of back to square one.
So I found that to be the one hurdle.
The second hurdle was establishing the key things that I want in that process, like a step-by-step breakdown.
So, just for example, in regards to the emails, we work with...
Agency.
The agency handles the automation for us.
We're looking to bring it internally, but for a while, the agency handles the emails.
So what they would do is they'll send a test email, and then I'll review the test email.
And then they would make some changes if requested, and then they would just go ahead and launch it.
But sometimes they would forget the changes or things are launching correctly.

So my thing was we have to build out a process, a QA sort of process where it's copywriter, comes to me, goes to the agency, goes back to me, goes back to the agency tool and get the perfect sort of email.
And that just takes time.
then, so that was, you know, that just naturally takes time because you have to work with a whole bunch of people.
So I found that to be obviously the biggest thing.
Not necessarily a huge hurdle, but just something you have to work with in the role.

And then, you know, obviously you mentioned you used a GPT to work with your content a little bit.
So could you expand a little bit on that?
Talk to me about how you use AI tooling, you know, to improve that process and work.
Or increase your leverage, so to speak.
Sure.
So I use them in two really, two distinct ways.
First, it was creating the custom AI, as you know, I'm sure you would know, in custom, and you can create the custom chat GPT.
And the idea is, we feed it information.
So it's like, I want this sort of email, I want this sort language, and then you create something for you.
And then it's like, no, no, no, let's give it some more information.
So the idea was sort of like a constant feeding of information, seeing what it's giving you, fine-tuning it, and then doing it again.
That's sort of how I initially use it to build out the content.
So in particularly for emails, and now we're doing it for SEO, and now I've done it for blogs, where I'm like, so we have a lot of videos.
Like the head of the company produces a lot of videos for promoting like a topic.
You know, obviously, that's the idea.
So the idea would like feed it to YouTube videos.
So it's like, let's see if you can grab her language, sorry, I should say, the transcript of the YouTube video.
Grabbing her language, and like, let's incorporate that into the blog.
So we have a, so it's almost sounding like she's speaking directly to you through the blog.
So that's how one thing was like feeding it information and then fine tuning it as it was coming out.
And that's one step.
The second step is finding gaps.
So a lot of the time you can do your own research and you can do like competitive research and see what other people are doing and what you're missing out.
But sometimes the AI can give you a little like, a little thing that you just sort of didn't pick up on.
It's like, oh, I'm seeing this.
It's detected a little gap of something that I just didn't notice was there.
And I found that would be particularly helpful.
So it could be something like we're optimizing some new landing pages.
So the idea would be that I put it in.
I've done the competitive research.
I've done my SEO research.
I'm like, I think there's a few things missing that I'm just want to clear, make sure I've got everything in the box.
And that, for example, would have been, oh, you're missing a schema markup.
that's not
should be added to the page.
So I found it to be really useful and able to get information and find gaps in processes and in the work that we're doing.
Obviously, you have to go over, sometimes take it with a grain of salt and you've got to double check it yourself because not necessarily, it's always super reliable, but that's how I've used it.
Yeah.
As you were doing that, either in the gap analysis or the general stylistic adherence and all that stuff, what was your, what are some things you learned?
What are some things you picked up as you went through that?
About, I think, more specifically about using AI tooling, not about yourself, but more about like, what did you learn?
What did you pick up when dealing with AI systems and content and kind of learning?
Oh, so no, no, that's fine.
So I found that you always have to almost double check and triple check what content's being produced.
And particularly when it comes to sort of like blogs, because emails are very different, but blogs, it's very much has this pinched from somewhere.
Even though I have told it not to take it from anywhere, has it taken it from some random site somewhere?
Is there a duplication issue that can penalize us later?
Is this, because sometimes you'd be like, I've given you the tools, sorry, I've given you the information to create a checklist for how to help people that are suffering relationships, for example.
Does this reflect what it's actually we say as a brand?
Or has it taken it from a competitor?
The last thing you want is some competitor information on your own page.
So I found out that it's very much triple-checking the content to make sure it's right and it is coming from a reliable source.
That's the main thing I found.
It does provide a great guide in terms of gaps in which you can find and what it can tell you, but it's also, you have to triple-check that.
can't just take it for verbatim.
That was the number one thing I found.

But the product is a variety of tools, but the main one you would be operating day-to-day is a pipeline, or a series of pipelines.
Those pipelines are agentic workflows that are hooked together to perform a task.
The primary task, the primary work stream that we do is editorial content.
We do a variety of other work streams as well, operate the pipelines to do them.
But the editorial one is probably the one that's most familiar with you.
It's similar to the work that you do now, with a combination of human work, drafting, and writing, plus custom GPT or any other assistance there.
Pipelines are just machinery that we have built to add better rails, to add editorial guidance, and sort of an editorial brain.

So it's operating the pipeline, editing the output, and getting it ready for client presentation, and then also doing an iterative improvement on the pipeline, either via inputs, which are the things that are very straightforward to control.
It's the same instructions that gives you, like a junior writer or something, you would say, hey, you know what
I want you to do this.
I would like shorter here or longer here or whatever, you know?
So the part of it is instructions that you're giving it.
The second major component is the context that you give it.
It would be like documents that you're feeding a custom GPT.
Are those architected properly?
Do they introduce inherent conflict?
Are they, you know, articulated well enough for an LLM to pick up on, understand, organize, and then act on?

And then the third major component is more of a collaboration with the engineering department, where you're like, hey, you know, I'm pretty sure this input is articulated well, this context is articulated well, and it's failing in this pretty significant way, and we'd love for this to be better so that our post-processing or after the workflow period of time can be short, and therefore increasing leverage.
And that collaboration work is done with engineering.
So you would basically file a ticket.

need?

And that writer, if they're very experienced, would probably go, great, I understand what you mean, I already have a framework for that because I've done this hundreds or thousands of times.

You know, this is actually not supported by the body of content that is underneath it, or worst case scenario, of course, the title that the body does not deliver on the promise given in the title or topic, you know, things like that.

We're very transparent about it.

I can see the cluster of content that we proposed, and we have some topics within that cluster, and we're executing on that.

I don't know if this is really going to drive towards this goal.
This is more of a top of funnel topic or a mid funnel topic.

And in all reality, we should be leaning into these topics, which are just more granular, or I see what keywords you want to attack here, but I see an angle that's better for this, you know, that really would hit the same overall keyword structure and fill in that gap from an SEO or DO perspective, but then also actually drive towards their goal of qualified leads.

So there's still a conversation that happens there, but the client-facing stuff we found, it's just really hard to context switch between, hey, I'm on camera, I'm talking and building rapport and all of that.

And then have to go operate pipelines the next minute and think like an articulating engineer or content engineer or manager managing that pipeline and articulating its process and improving it, you know.

So this role is largely internal facing, although it is not, you know, it's not like you never ever talk to a client, maybe you will, you know, especially for project based stuff or whatever, but it's largely internal facing.
No, that's great to know.

Like obviously no queries with being a senior copywriter, have spoken to clients before because you've got to get that feel sort of what they want and, you know, work with them on like a one-on-one level.
In terms of content creation, are we looking at everything like emails, blogs, landing pages, is it a wide scope of things?
Or is it everything under the content umbrella gets created depending on what the client wants?
Or do we, do you guys specifically look at something?
I would love to say it's one thing, but every client is just a little bit of a wild card when you're working on it.
You know, the vast majority of what we produce, I would file under, let's call it organic editorial content.
So this would be something like a blog post or an article that lives on a client's site.
We do, however, have some newsletter engagements.
We will run their newsletter, like on Beehive for them.
This is this, most of the clients that we do this for did not previously have a newsletter.
So like taking over a newsletter and doing it, we don't really do, have not done a lot of that.
Doesn't mean we can't.
The cool thing about the pipelines is that they are relatively malleable.
So we can create a pipeline to generate just about anything.
But our two major work streams, the ones I would say you would be most likely to encounter, editorial, as I discussed, and then programmatic SEO or PSEO.
And the programmatic SEO would fall into the realm of something like dozens of glossary pages, a pricing directory.
Oh, okay.
You know, things like this that we're able to pipe, say, a half dozen different pieces of data and maybe marry that with a small editorial component as well.

We're doing a pricing index for one of our clients right now where they're basically a pricing model index specifically that is articulating every AI company in the space, the pricing model that they use.
Obviously, the history of that model, that sort of thing, so you can see pricing history to create an index that can be a link magnet and traffic magnet, of course, authority building.
And then in that, we are also, we've built into that an editorial component, which is their take.
So it's like, okay, we've ingested a bunch of internal documentation about what they think about these kinds of pricing models, a variety of them, all the major ones.
And then we are synthesizing their take using our pipelines so that the take gets injected, you know, organically after that stage.
So you have the data, which is obviously scraped and then fact-checked.
Or researched and then fact-checked combination of things by the pipeline.

And then the pipeline also says, cool, I have context, contextual artifacts, and instructions about how to parse their internal documentation about these things and synthesize a small editorial take on it so that that goes on the page and it feels more lived in.
You know, it's not just, cool, here's a directory of data that we scraped.
That's fine.
You know, a lot of people can do that in one way or another.
Usually it's just like raw manual labor, gets it done.

But with our pipelines, it's able to integrate or, you know, interleave these sort of editorial or synthesized bits with data scraping and fact-checking and then layout and shipping, you know, or publishing all to the client.
And that enables us to do things like publish a couple hundred pages in very rapid succession for a client that has basically like an editorial take.

wouldn't want to sugarcoat it and say that we have, like, an enormously long ramp for you to, like, you know, hey, cool, in six months, you're going to start producing client work.
